methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $160M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Quantum Motion is a Series C quantum computing company that has raised $160M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comp universe of 11 peers across quantum computing—3 public (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave) and 8 private entities including Quantinuum and Infleqtion—though the sector remains nascent with limited direct comparables at identical stage and scale.

The Stage-Progression method is the primary applicable approach, using total capital raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical for Series C advancement, yielding $272M. The Peer-Median method was not applied due to insufficient depth: while 7 same-sector peers exist in the database, filtering for matching stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier produced fewer than three datapoints, making sector-wide median comparison unreliable and potentially misleading.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $231M (low) to $313M (high), with a median point of $272M. This single-method triangulation reflects the constraints of an immature sector where comparable transaction data remains sparse. Key uncertainties include the appropriate step-up multiplier for quantum computing specifically versus broader venture stages, the relevance of public market multiples given the pre-commercialization status of most peers, and the binary nature of technical feasibility risk in hardware advancement. Investors should weight this valuation alongside detailed technical validation and capital efficiency metrics given the high execution risk inherent to the sector.
